Title: Exploring Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato biofilm-like aggregate formation in susceptible and PTI-responding Arabidopsis thaliana

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense signaling; antimicrobial and antibiofilm activity in leaf intercellular spaces
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of SID2 reduces plant-produced salicylic acid, partially restores Pst mutant multiplication and aggregate formation, and weakens PTI-associated suppression of bacterial biofilm-like aggregates
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Use of SA-deficient sid2-2 mutant plants; bacterial growth assays in planta; fluorescence microscopy quantification of aggregate number and size; SA biosensor assay in intercellular washing fluid
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. sid2-2 plants, deficient in SA biosynthesis, allowed greater multiplication and larger/more numerous aggregates of Pst ΔalgD ΔalgU ΔmucAB than wild-type Col-0. 2. Pst ΔalgD alone formed many large aggregates in sid2-2 but fewer/smaller aggregates in Col-0, supporting a negative effect of plant-produced SA on aggregate formation. 3. A modest PTI response remained in sid2-2, indicating PTI is largely but not exclusively SA dependent.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Pst inoculation; flg22 pretreatment to induce PTI
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: SA deficiency compromises defense and increases bacterial success in planta
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Supports maintaining or enhancing SA biosynthesis capacity to suppress pathogen biofilm formation and improve disease resistance
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: SID2 is a plant defense/metabolite biosynthesis gene rather than a bacterial nutrient-metabolism gene; included because SA is the focal metabolite causally linked to bacterial aggregate suppression in the Results.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defense signaling; antimicrobial and antibiofilm activity in leaf intercellular spaces during PTI
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Functional FLS2 is required for flg22-induced PTI-associated intercellular SA accumulation and strong suppression of Pst aggregate formation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Use of PTI-defective fls2 mutant plants; flg22 pretreatment; in planta bacterial quantification; aggregate imaging and counting; SA biosensor assay in IWF
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. flg22 treatment reduced Pst growth and aggregate formation in Col-0 but not in fls2. 2. flg22-treated Col-0 accumulated high intercellular SA, whereas fls2 accumulated little SA. 3. These results link FLS2-dependent PTI activation to SA accumulation and reduced bacterial biofilm-like aggregate formation.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: flg22 pretreatment followed by Pst inoculation
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of FLS2 abolishes flg22-induced PTI and permits high pathogen growth and aggregation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Supports strengthening PAMP perception pathways to enhance SA-associated suppression of pathogen biofilms
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: FLS2 is upstream of SA accumulation and was functionally central in the Results, although not itself a metabolic enzyme.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato in Arabidopsis infection system
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato in Arabidopsis thaliana
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: GFP-expressing Pst; ΔalgD; ΔalgD ΔalgU ΔmucAB
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established in this paper; treated as required alginate biosynthesis enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Biofilm matrix component contributing to bacterial aggregate formation and protection against plant defense
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Bacterial extracellular matrix in leaf intercellular spaces
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of algD reduced aggregate number and size, especially in SA-producing plants, but alone did not significantly reduce bacterial multiplication in planta
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Deletion mutant analysis (ΔalgD); comparison of bacterial growth in Col-0 and sid2-2; fluorescence microscopy quantification of aggregate numbers and size
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Pst ΔalgD formed fewer and smaller aggregates than wild-type Pst in Col-0. 2. In sid2-2, aggregate formation by ΔalgD was largely restored, indicating alginate is particularly important under plant SA pressure. 3. ΔalgD alone multiplied similarly to wild-type Pst, showing alginate affects aggregation more clearly than bulk multiplication in this context.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Comparison in SA-producing Col-0 versus SA-deficient sid2-2 plants
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of alginate biosynthesis reduces aggregate formation and may increase sensitivity to plant-produced SA
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential anti-virulence target: disrupting alginate biosynthesis may reduce pathogen biofilm establishment, especially when host SA defenses are intact
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper studies pathogen fitness rather than host nutritional quality; alginate is a bacterial extracellular polysaccharide, not a plant nutrient metabolite.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species: Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato in Arabidopsis infection system
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato in Arabidopsis thaliana
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: GFP-expressing Pst; ΔalgU ΔmucAB; ΔalgD ΔalgU ΔmucAB
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation/reprogramming of alginate biosynthesis and virulence-associated genes
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Alginate biosynthesis and biofilm-like aggregate formation
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: alginate biosynthesis genes including algD; hrpL; some T3SS effector genes such as HopY1, AvrPtoB, AvrPto, HopE1 (as discussed in paper)
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes expression of alginate biosynthesis genes and some virulence genes; proposed to down-regulate flagellin genes
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Antagonized by MucAB anti-sigma components as implied by mutant genotype
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Important regulator of biofilm formation because loss of AlgU strongly reduces aggregate number and size in planta
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Biofilm matrix support and protection against host defense
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Bacterial extracellular matrix in leaf intercellular spaces
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of AlgU reduced Pst aggregate formation and bacterial multiplication in planta, but did not increase intercellular SA accumulation relative to wild-type Pst
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis using ΔalgU ΔmucAB and ΔalgD ΔalgU ΔmucAB strains; in planta bacterial growth assays; fluorescence microscopy-based aggregate quantification; SA biosensor assay in IWF
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Pst mutants lacking AlgU formed fewer and smaller aggregates than wild-type Pst. 2. The quadruple mutant lacking both AlgU and AlgD formed even fewer aggregates than the AlgU mutant, indicating contributions from both regulator and alginate biosynthesis. 3. Col-0 accumulated similarly low SA in response to wild-type Pst and algU mutants, suggesting AlgU is not required for suppression of SA-mediated plant defense. 4. AlgU therefore promotes aggregate formation mainly through regulation of bacterial traits such as alginate production and possibly flagellin repression rather than through modulation of host SA accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Infection in Col-0 and sid2-2; comparison under PTI-related host defense contexts
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of AlgU reduces virulence-associated aggregate formation and bacterial growth in planta
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential anti-virulence target for limiting pathogen biofilms by disrupting global regulatory control of alginate-associated aggregation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although AlgU also affects T3SS-associated virulence, this paper's experimentally emphasized phenotype is reduced biofilm-like aggregate formation. Epistasis with algD suggests both transcriptional regulation and alginate synthesis contribute.
